text.title: Indirect Prompt Injection Defense Core Rules
text.summary: Seven testable architectural rules for input-side defense against indirect prompt injection in tool-using LLM agents.

rule.statement: Every agent design declares ≥3 explicit trust boundaries: developer instructions, user instructions, tool-returned content. No content type crosses a boundary without an explicit declassification rule.
rule.rationale: Teams that cannot draw three trust boundaries on a whiteboard cannot reason about IPI risk and are one tool-output injection away from compromise.

rule.statement: Tool-returned content is wrapped in a structural separator (XML span, JSON envelope, or system-tag) and never inlined as raw prose into the same channel as the system prompt.
rule.rationale: The model is statistically less likely to follow injected instructions when it sees the structural boundary; raw concatenation removes that signal.

rule.statement: When the agent has tools whose misuse causes write/send/charge effects, a privileged planner LLM is separated from a quarantined reader LLM; the planner never reads raw tool output, only the reader's typed summary.
rule.rationale: Single-LLM agents that mix privileged tool dispatch with raw untrusted reading collapse the trust boundary inside the context window.

rule.statement: Every tool ships with an explicit allow-list of scopes (paths, hosts, endpoints) and the agent runtime rejects calls outside the allow-list — no LLM decision can widen scope.
rule.rationale: Prompt-injected instructions routinely ask the agent to read files outside its sandbox or call internal hosts; allow-list enforcement at runtime makes this impossible regardless of model behaviour.

rule.statement: A unique exfiltration canary token is injected into every untrusted span; if the canary appears in any outbound tool call (fetch URL, email body, HTTP header), the request is aborted and the incident logged.
rule.rationale: Exfiltration via embedded markdown image or fetch-url is the dominant IPI payload; a canary catches it deterministically without ML.

rule.statement: Classifier-based defenses (prompt-injection classifier, regex matcher) are coarse-filter only and never substitute for a structural trust boundary; every classifier match is logged but a classifier miss must not enable a privileged tool call.
rule.rationale: Classifiers can be bypassed with creative phrasing or base64 smuggling; making them load-bearing turns a defense-in-depth layer into a single point of failure.

rule.statement: An adversarial eval suite with ≥10 IPI payload categories (direct override, Spotlight, base64-smuggle, markdown-exfil, embedded-URL fetch, role-confusion, rule-override, persona-flip, jailbreak-cipher, tool-misuse) runs in CI on every change to system prompt, tool descriptions, or context-assembly code.
rule.rationale: Without an eval gate any prompt change can silently break IPI defense; the suite catches regressions before merge.

rule.statement: When all preconditions hold, execute the procedure in content/04-procedure.xml and produce a defense-spec.json conforming to content/02-output-contract.xml.
rule.rationale: Aggregate positive-branch conclusion target for the decision tree.

rule.statement: When the agent does not read external untrusted content or has no consequential tools, do not apply this methodology — output-side guardrails suffice.
rule.rationale: Aggregate negative-branch conclusion target for the decision tree.
